content.assessment: The status-quo trajectory is politically feasible in the narrow sense that it requires no new legislative coalition to sustain it — it is already the default operating mode, backed by the entrenched KLIK centralization structure and the alternative-operator coalition of church takeovers. However, its legitimacy is uneven and reactive: survival depends on ad hoc mobilization capacity (petitions, demonstrations) rather than durable rule-based authority, producing an arbitrary distribution of political costs and benefits across similarly-situated villages.
opportunities: Existing sectoral mobilization capacity (teacher/parent/student coalitions) could be channeled into future reform advocacy rather than only reactive defense (F-live-political_feasibility-08)
strengths: Requires no new enacting coalition; persists by default administrative inertia (F-live-political_feasibility-01)
Church-sector expansion provides an already-functioning alternative political coalition absorbing some closure pressure (F-live-political_feasibility-02)
Demonstrated capacity for informal reversal after local political mobilization, showing a built-in (if unreliable) safety valve (F-live-political_feasibility-07)
threats: Persistent budget deficits in the centralized system (KLIK deficits since inception) create ongoing pressure for further ad hoc closures regardless of political resistance (F-live-political_feasibility-06)
Nationwide teacher mobilization capacity signals rising political cost for any future reform attempt, including possible tightening of the status quo (F-live-political_feasibility-08)

Long-run historical pattern of repeated regime-driven swings in school-network size suggests the current equilibrium is inherently unstable and could be displaced by a future top-down centralizing or decentralizing shock (F-live-political_feasibility-09)
weaknesses: Reliance on rare local social capital for survival produces politically indefensible, inconsistent outcomes across comparable villages (F-live-political_feasibility-03)
Centralized KLIK decision-making removes locally accountable mayors from the loop, weakening the chain of democratic accountability for closures (F-live-political_feasibility-01)
No codified appeal mechanism means legitimacy rests entirely on the salience and organizing capacity of each protest, which is not evenly distributed (F-live-political_feasibility-07)
